item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … This entire skip is just someone who understands machine learning's limitations venting, and y'know what? I'm okay with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this very topical. Also, congrats on +100!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The numbers on this are completely unbelievable. Where are all these anomalous memes coming from? How many anomalous terrorist hacker groups could there be? How are these memes escaping containment so quick on system failure if they’re totally contained up to that point? It’s almost parodic. The execution is interesting, but would have been perfectly cromulent at 100,000th the scale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry but this just wasn't for me all the containment procedures and the collapsibles were very boring to read and by the end i still have no idea what the a anomally even was

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really my style of SCP, per se, but +1'd for the craft on display here. I like the commitment to "internal technical report" format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1 for reasons of this being too dry and uninteresting; I get that it's basically a fake actual research paper in neural nets, but…geez.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a new one after 6th? I totally skip the issue #'s (or was it volume? The ones in parens) and definitely don't do double spaces. Dirty little secret: as long as they're mostly correct and have the DoI #, unless the journal has their own guidelines, people mostly don't care. I'm not saying you should do them wrong, people deserve credit. But it happens.

Also noting that Kate McTiriss popularized the SCP journal citation deal, because that's what caused me to join the wiki. (Not to take away from the current author, these are very neat.)

To give some actual critique, I'm mostly with LordMetalton and CWW. The back and forth is really interesting on this (the poison image and variance versus bias discussion was neat, although I need to wrap my head around that in SDT terms) but I really don't understand how this is an anomaly. Bardeen got away with that exact issue because it gave more room to a very touching personal narrative, and even Bardeen was paratech. This doesn't have that. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love hard science like this one, even if a decent chunk of it went over my head. I did know a bit of neural net stuff from a few YT videos.

But my overall favorite part of this is some group poisoning the algorithms like 4chan poisons CAPTCHAs and the like. I find it hilarious how the Foundation has to deal with groups like that (a la GAW.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made a minor revision to the years of some of the paper citations, because in my head the Foundation should be at least a few years ahead of real-world technology.

item.title: Awwww
item.content:encoded: I thought this article was actually gonna be about memes…… but apart from that HOLY FLYING FUCK IS THAT A LONG ARTICLE JESUS TAKE MY +1 YOU DETAILED BASTARD!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
at what point do you decide to just digitize someone's visual cortex with tech i'm sure the foundation has somewhere and run it in an emulator, looking for unexpected variations

or would you run into the halting problem doing that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reddy, S., Balasubramanian, R., (2015). Containment of SCP-████ using backpropagation through time. Terminal: an SCP Foundation Journal, 18(56), 46-61. Retrieved from SCP Foundation Research Database. (Accession No. 6356765)

i upvoted here and that upvote held strong through the rest of the piece, especially since I love anything that addresses catastrophes that occur because a neural network fucks up and people were relying on it for mission-critical shit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can semi-confirm: I've studied computer science and I'm a (former?) amateur programmer, and I could follow along pretty well. I can't implement any of this stuff, of course, but I can understand what's going on at each step.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"What would the Foundation actually need to do in order to build computer systems that can identify and filter cognitohazards?" Well, first you gather Literally A Billion Memes…

I'll admit it's probably because I'm a HUGE NERD but I really liked this. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember seeing this in chat. I did know a little tiny bit about computers, so I offered to review it. And I remember barfing rainbows. So I excused myself out :p (No, actually, I'm not that dickish, I did have to go). But now seeing it on the mainsite and finally able to read it fully (still barfed rainbows tho) I can say, good job.

Shut up and take my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it all comes down to which citation style one is using. I won't put citations as footnotes if I use APA, usually using them as bibliography along with bracket citations.

All things considered, I do regard the Foundation to use their own citation style.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy footnotes that are formatted like real citations batman.

Unless you're a psyc student and using the new APA manual! Issue number italicization is weird and I forget if we even need to include them anymore! Two spaces after full stops!

aaaaAAAaAAaAaAAaaAAaAaAAaAaaaaaa

That said, author, I'm impressed by the footnotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm inclined to agree, though with the caveat that I don't actually know enough about computer science to be confident in that agreement.

Moreover, for me this sorta sits at the intersection of SCP-2223, SCP-1718, and SCP-2163. Not too similar to any one of those to justify its existence, but taken together, this one doesn't have much to offer me that I can't get elsewhere.

And, less importantly, when I saw the title "Literally a Billion Memes", I expected something more exciting than this. Alas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the technical detail here, but one issue I have is that it doesn't seem like this should have an SCP classification. The tech they have going into this doesn't seem too much more advanced than anything Google or IBM is already doing IRL, they just trained it to detect things that are anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is well written, technically competent and an interesting idea, but I found it horrifically tedious to read. So much here felt like unnecessarily added detail for the sake of adding detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is actually pretty bog-standard CS. A bit in the experimental/research area, but this is kind of what programmers do all day. Especially the good ones.

EDIT: This is not easy computer science, but it's nothing that's on the insane bleeding edge righ tnow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good lord, do you actually do this stuff professionally? I only hobby in it, but as near as I can tell you absolutely knocked the science out of the park in terms of accuracy, as well as added a nice dash of horror (i.e. it's gonna fail unpredictably and it's prone to adversarial failures) based on the real-world limitations of machine learning, and only had to add a little hand-wavey science (machines being able to "understand" memes) to make it work.

I was expecting some sort of "powered by tortured orphan brains" as the final twist, and I was pleasantly surprised that you kept it relevant to the subject. Excellent!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never in my life will I be able to read something like this if it is printed or written on a long-ranged paper.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember reading the draft

amazing job, a +1 =P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy footnotes batman. Holy footnotes that are formatted like real citations batman. Take my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neural net diagram image was generated from a python script in the open-source machine learning library Caffe from the Berkeley Vision and Learning Center using the schematic of GoogLeNet (replicated by BVLC with help from Szegedy from a paper by Google and some universities), and then modified.

The adversarial images example with the schoolbus is actually credited in-article, but it’s from the Szegedy et al. paper “Intriguing Properties of Neural Networks” on arXiv under a Creative Commons license: https://arxiv.org/abs/1312.6199

Thanks to Leveritas on the forums, and 8ync, Jishwa, Uasciovuza, BenjaminStokes, DrMagnus, Rimple, Herlequine, Roget, and SleepyIsHollow (and FoldingPapers, sorry!) from chat for critiquing and giving feedback.
